ETC. Vs. RESPONDENT: UNION OF INDIA AND ORS., ETC. DATE OF JUDGMENT29/10/1991 BENCH: RAY, B.C. BENCH: RAY, B.C. SHARMA, L.M. VENKATACHALLIAH, M.N. VER...
citations_found.ai_summary:
Here is a 2-3 sentence summary of the Indian legal case:

The dispute was about the removal of a Judge of the Supreme Court, with the Sub-Committee on Judicial Accountability seeking to investigate the Judge's alleged misconduct and the Union of India arguing that the motion for removal had lapsed due to the dissolution of the Lok Sabha. The court ultimately decided that the motion for removal did not lapse and that the Judges (Inquiry) Act, 1968, was a law under Article 124(5) of the Constitution, which excluded the operation of the doctrine of lapse.

citations_found.ai_summary:
Here's a 2-3 sentence summary of the case:

The dispute in this case was about the confirmation of a death sentence imposed on Nabin Dehury by the Additional Sessions Judge, Kuchinda, for a crime committed in 2020. The Orissa High Court was asked to confirm the death sentence through a reference under Section 366 of the Code of Criminal Procedure, 1973. The court ultimately decided to confirm the death sentence imposed on Nabin Dehury.

citations_found.ai_summary:
Here is a 2-3 sentence summary of the Indian legal case:

The dispute in Chander Parkash Soni vs R.S Grewal & Ors was about the ownership and possession of a suit property, with the defendants (tenants) being sued for possession by the plaintiffs. The court ultimately decided that the suit for possession was not maintainable, as the defendants were put into possession of the shops as tenants by the original owner, Smt. Shivdev Kaur. The court's decision disposed of two Regular Second Appeals filed by the defendants.

citations_found.ai_summary:
Here's a 2-3 sentence summary of the Indian legal case:

The dispute in Uma Sashi Devi (D) Th.Lrs vs Dibakar Banerjee & Ors was about the ownership of properties left behind by their mother, Smt. Mrinalini Devi, with the sister (Uma Sashi Devi) claiming a share in the properties settled by their mother through two registered Deeds of Settlement. The Supreme Court ultimately decided that the properties described in Schedules A, C, and D fell exclusively to the share of the brother, while the sister had a share in the properties described in Schedules B and E.

citations_found.ai_summary:
Here is a 2-3 sentence summary of the Indian legal case:

The dispute in this case was about the decriminalization of consensual same-sex relationships in India, with the petitioners challenging the constitutionality of Section 377 of the Indian Penal Code. The Supreme Court, in a landmark judgment, upheld the decision in Navtej Singh Johar (2018) and held that consensual same-sex relationships between adults are not a criminal offense. This judgment effectively decriminalized same-sex relationships in India.
